---
title: "Ch 4 — Parallels"
subtitle: "When two lines never meet — and why every triangle has 180°"
lesson-id: "kg1.parallels"
tier: "kg1"
kiselev-book: "Geometry, Book I (Planimetry)"
kiselev-chapter: 4
kiselev-chapter-title: "Parallels"
kiselev-sections: "Planimetry §§75–96 (parallels, transversals, the angle sum)"
prerequisites: [kg1.perpendiculars, kg1.triangle-congruence]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: [game-dev, research]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you name the angles formed when two lines cross — adjacent,
   vertical, supplementary? See
   [`cbse9.lines-and-angles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/06-lines-and-angles/index.qmd).
2. Do you accept that two lines crossing make **vertical angles
   equal**?
3. Can you describe what *parallel* means in everyday language —
   railway tracks, ruled lines on a page?
4. Are you comfortable with the word *theorem* meaning "a statement
   we prove from the axioms and earlier theorems"?

If three yeses, proceed.
:::

## Touch

Two horizontal lines, drawn close together. A third line — the
**transversal** — cuts across both. Drag the transversal: watch the
eight angles update.

```{ojs}
viewof slope = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1.2, step: 0.1, label: "Transversal slope"})
```

```{ojs}
viewof xshift = Inputs.range([-2, 2], {value: 0, step: 0.1, label: "Transversal offset"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const w = 460, h = 320, padX = 40, padY = 40;
  const xMin = -5, xMax = 5;
  const xS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([xMin, xMax]).range([padX, w - padX]);
  const yS = d3.scaleLinear().domain([-3, 3]).range([h - padY, padY]);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", w).attr("height", h)
    .style("background", "#fafafa").style("border", "1px solid #ddd");

  // two parallel horizontal lines at y = -1 and y = 1
  for (const y of [-1, 1]) {
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(xMin)).attr("x2", xS(xMax))
      .attr("y1", yS(y)).attr("y2", yS(y))
      .attr("stroke", "#26a").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  }
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX + 5).attr("y", yS(1) - 5)
    .attr("font-size", 11).text("line a");
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX + 5).attr("y", yS(-1) - 5)
    .attr("font-size", 11).text("line b");

  // transversal: y = slope * (x - xshift), drawn long
  const xL = xMin, xR = xMax;
  const yL = slope * (xL - xshift), yR = slope * (xR - xshift);
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", xS(xL)).attr("x2", xS(xR))
    .attr("y1", yS(yL)).attr("y2", yS(yR))
    .attr("stroke", "#a26").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", w - padX - 60).attr("y", yS(yR) - 5)
    .attr("font-size", 11).text("transversal");

  // mark intersection points
  const ix1 = xshift + 1 / slope;   // y = 1 cut
  const ix2 = xshift - 1 / slope;   // y = -1 cut
  const angleDeg = Math.round(Math.atan(slope) * 180 / Math.PI * 10) / 10;

  if (Math.abs(slope) > 0.05) {
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", xS(ix1)).attr("cy", yS(1))
      .attr("r", 4).attr("fill", "#333");
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", xS(ix2)).attr("cy", yS(-1))
      .attr("r", 4).attr("fill", "#333");
    svg.append("text").attr("x", xS(ix1) + 8).attr("y", yS(1) - 8)
      .attr("font-size", 11).text(`A`);
    svg.append("text").attr("x", xS(ix2) + 8).attr("y", yS(-1) - 8)
      .attr("font-size", 11).text(`B`);
  }

  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY - 10)
    .attr("font-size", 13)
    .text(`Transversal angle (with horizontal): ${angleDeg}°`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", padX).attr("y", padY + 8)
    .attr("font-size", 12)
    .text(`The four "upper" angles at A equal the four "lower" angles at B.`);
  return svg.node();
}
```

The two horizontal lines never meet — they are **parallel**. The
transversal makes the same angle at $A$ as at $B$.

## Wonder

1. Suppose I drew two lines that *look* parallel. How could I be
   *sure* they are parallel — without extending them to infinity?
2. The transversal cuts angles at $A$ and at $B$. Some are equal,
   some sum to $180°$. **Can you spot a pattern in which is which?**
3. If the four upper angles at $A$ have measures $\alpha, \beta,
   \gamma, \delta$, do the four at $B$ have the same multiset of
   measures? Why?
4. Consider any triangle. Drag a parallel through one vertex — what
   do you notice about the three angles of the triangle and the
   straight angle at that vertex?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(2) **Alternate-interior** and **corresponding** angles are equal;
**co-interior** angles sum to $180°$. (3) Yes — the transversal
makes the same set of four angles at each crossing of a parallel.
(4) The three triangle-angles together fill the straight angle —
this is the proof of the *triangle-angle-sum theorem*.
:::

## Discover

We work in classical Euclidean style: each step uses only earlier
results.

### Problem 1 — Definition

**State precisely** what *parallel* means. Two phrasings are
candidates: *(a)* lines that never meet; *(b)* lines that are
everywhere the same distance apart. Are they equivalent?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Both are tempting. Equivalence requires a proof — and that proof
needs the angle theory we are about to develop.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Kiselev takes **(a)** as the definition: *parallel lines are two lines
in the same plane that do not meet, however far they are extended.*
He then **proves** (b) as a theorem (Planimetry §86): parallels are
equidistant. The two phrasings are equivalent in Euclidean geometry,
but only after a proof.
</details>

### Problem 2 — A construction of parallels

Given a line $\ell$ and a point $P$ not on $\ell$, **construct** a
line through $P$ that does not meet $\ell$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Drop a perpendicular from $P$ to $\ell$. Then erect a perpendicular
to *that* perpendicular at $P$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Drop perpendicular $PF$ from $P$ to $\ell$ ($F \in \ell$). Through
$P$, erect a line $m$ perpendicular to $PF$. Then $m$ does not meet
$\ell$ — for if it did, at some point $Q$, the triangle $PFQ$ would
have **two right angles** ($\angle PFQ$ and $\angle FPQ$), giving
$180°$ between just two angles, leaving none for $\angle PQF$.
**Existence is proved.** (Planimetry §76.)
</details>

### Problem 3 — The parallel postulate (Euclid V)

The construction above produces *one* line through $P$ that does not
meet $\ell$. **Could there be more than one?**

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Euclid's Fifth Postulate** asserts: *exactly one* parallel through
$P$. This is independent of the other axioms — there are non-Euclidean
geometries (hyperbolic, with infinitely many parallels; spherical,
with none) in which it fails. In *this* lesson we accept the Fifth
Postulate as a working axiom; everything that follows depends on it.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Alternate-interior angles

A transversal cuts parallel lines $a$ and $b$ at $A$ and $B$. Number
the four angles at $A$ as $1, 2, 3, 4$ and at $B$ as $5, 6, 7, 8$,
matching the figure below. Show that **alternate-interior angles are
equal:** $\angle 3 = \angle 6$.

```
              1 \\ 2
        ───────A────── (line a)
              4 \\ 3
                 \\
              5 \\ 6
        ───────B────── (line b)
              8 \\ 7
```

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Argue by contradiction. Suppose $\angle 3 \ne \angle 6$. Then one of
the angles (say $\angle 3$) is larger; rotate the transversal at $B$
to make $\angle 6$ equal $\angle 3$, and see what goes wrong.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Suppose $\angle 3 \ne \angle 6$. WLOG $\angle 3 > \angle 6$. Then a
line $b'$ at $B$ making angle $\angle 3$ with the transversal would
*also* not meet $a$ (by the same argument as Problem 2 generalised),
contradicting the **uniqueness** of the parallel through $B$. So
$\angle 3 = \angle 6$. *(Kiselev's classical proof; Planimetry §80.)*
</details>

### Problem 5 — Corresponding angles

Show that **corresponding angles** are equal: $\angle 2 = \angle 6$
in the same figure.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\angle 2$ and $\angle 3$ are vertical (or supplementary, depending
on labelling) — link via Problem 4.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle 2 = \angle 4$ (vertical angles, from earlier chapter).
$\angle 4$ and $\angle 3$ are supplementary
($\angle 4 + \angle 3 = 180°$). Likewise $\angle 6 + \angle 7 =
180°$ and $\angle 6 = \angle 8$ (vertical). From Problem 4,
$\angle 3 = \angle 6$, hence $\angle 4 = \angle 7$, etc. The
*corresponding* pair $\angle 2 = \angle 6$ falls out.
</details>

### Problem 6 — Triangle-angle-sum

Let $\triangle ABC$ be any triangle. Prove that
$\angle A + \angle B + \angle C = 180°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Through the vertex $C$, draw a line parallel to $AB$. Use Problem 4
twice.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $m$ be the line through $C$ parallel to $AB$ (exists by Problem
2; unique by Problem 3). The angles on one side of $m$ at $C$ are
three: an angle equal to $\angle A$ (alternate interior with the
transversal $AC$); the original $\angle C$ of the triangle; an angle
equal to $\angle B$ (alternate interior with the transversal $BC$).
The three together form a **straight angle** along $m$, so
$\angle A + \angle B + \angle C = 180°$. **QED.**
*(Kiselev's classical proof; Planimetry §89.)*
</details>

### Problem 7 — A converse

Suppose two lines $a$ and $b$ are cut by a transversal so that
**alternate-interior angles are equal**. Prove that $a \parallel b$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
If $a$ and $b$ met at some point $Q$, the transversal together with
$a$ and $b$ would form a triangle with two given equal alternate-
interior angles. By the exterior-angle theorem (earlier in Kiselev,
§78), an exterior angle of a triangle is *strictly greater* than the
remote interior angle — contradicting the equality the hypothesis
gives. Hence $a$ and $b$ never meet — i.e., $a \parallel b$.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
**Lobachevsky and the Russian discovery of non-Euclidean geometry.**
For two thousand years mathematicians tried — and failed — to *prove*
the [parallel postulate](/glossary.qmd#parallel-postulate) from the
other axioms. The Italian Jesuit **Giovanni Saccheri** (1733) came
heartbreakingly close to a proof; in fact he had already constructed
a non-Euclidean geometry but could not bring himself to believe in
it. The breakthrough came from **Nikolai Lobachevsky**, professor
at Kazan University, who in 1829 published a system in which
*infinitely many* parallels pass through a point off a line —
*hyperbolic geometry*. The Hungarian **János Bolyai** discovered the
same independently in 1832. Their work was greeted with confusion
and (in Lobachevsky's case) public ridicule; **Gauss** privately
agreed but never published. Today hyperbolic and spherical geometries
are the foundation of general relativity. *Kiselev's planimetry sits
inside one corner — the Euclidean corner — of a much larger geometric
landscape, and that landscape is part of Russia's mathematical
heritage.*
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**The [parallel postulate](/glossary.qmd#parallel-postulate) is what
makes triangle angles sum to exactly $180°$.** Change the postulate
(allow many parallels, or none) and the angle sum changes too —
*less than $180°$* on a saddle, *more than $180°$* on a sphere. The
flatness of school geometry is a *choice*, not a necessity.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Definition.** Two lines $a, b$ in a plane are **parallel**
($a \parallel b$) if they do not meet, however far extended.

**Postulate (Euclid V).** Through a point not on a given line, there
is **exactly one** parallel to that line.

**Theorem (alternate-interior angles).** If $a \parallel b$ and a
transversal cuts them, then alternate-interior angles are equal.
*Conversely*, if alternate-interior angles are equal then
$a \parallel b$.

**Theorem (triangle-angle-sum).** The three angles of any triangle
sum to a straight angle ($180°$).

:::

A verse:

> *Two lines never meeting — that's parallel, and one rule reigns.*
> *Through a point off a line, one such line, no more, remains.*
> *A transversal makes alternates equal, that's how we agree —*
> *And the three angles of any triangle sum to one-eighty.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> In the figure below, $AB \parallel CD$. The transversal $EF$ cuts
> $AB$ at $G$ and $CD$ at $H$. If $\angle AGE = 65°$, find every
> angle at $G$ and at $H$.

At $G$: $\angle AGE = 65°$, so its **vertical** angle $\angle BGF =
65°$. Its **supplements** are $\angle AGF = \angle BGE = 115°$.

At $H$: corresponding to $\angle AGE$ is $\angle CHG = 65°$ (parallels
+ transversal). Then the four angles at $H$ are $65°, 115°, 65°, 115°$
in the same arrangement.

**All eight angles** are either $65°$ or $115°$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One acute angle
formed is $42°$. List all eight angles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Each crossing has angles $42°, 138°, 42°, 138°$. Both crossings have
the same set, by Problems 4–5 above.
</details>

**W2.** A triangle has angles $40°$ and $75°$. Find the third.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$180° - 40° - 75° = 65°$.
</details>

**W3.** Two angles of a triangle are equal, and the third is $50°$.
Find the equal pair.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$180° - 50° = 130°$, split equally: $65°$ each.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** In a triangle the second angle is twice the first, and the
third is three times the first. Find all three.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let the first be $x$. Sum: $x + 2x + 3x = 6x = 180°$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = 30°$. Angles: $30°, 60°, 90°$ — a $30$–$60$–$90$ triangle.
</details>

**P2.** Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal so that the two
**co-interior** angles are in ratio $2 : 3$. Find them.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Co-interior angles sum to $180°$ (use the alt-interior theorem +
linear pair).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2x + 3x = 180°$, so $x = 36°$. Angles $72°$ and $108°$.
</details>

**P3.** Show that the **exterior angle** of a triangle equals the sum
of the two remote interior angles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
At a vertex $C$, the exterior angle is $180° - \angle C$. By the
angle-sum theorem, $\angle A + \angle B = 180° - \angle C$. Equate:
exterior angle at $C$ $= \angle A + \angle B$. (*This is the
exterior-angle theorem in its sharper form*; Planimetry §90.)
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** $ABCD$ is a quadrilateral. Prove that the sum of its four
interior angles is $360°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Draw a diagonal — say $AC$ — splitting the quadrilateral into two
triangles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Diagonal $AC$ produces $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle ACD$. Each
has angle sum $180°$. Total $360°$. The four interior angles of
$ABCD$ partition into the angles of these two triangles
(no overlap, no leftover). Hence $\angle A + \angle B + \angle C +
\angle D = 360°$. **QED.**
</details>

**S2.** Prove the converse of the corresponding-angles theorem: if a
transversal makes equal corresponding angles with two lines, the
lines are parallel.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Equal corresponding angles + supplementary linear pair $\Rightarrow$
equal alternate-interior angles. Then by Problem 7 the lines are
parallel.
</details>

### Kiselev-style problems

Three problems in Kiselev's classical style, adapted from
Givental, *Planimetry*, §§88–94.

**K1.** In a triangle, the angles are in arithmetic progression.
Show that the **middle** angle is exactly $60°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let the three angles be $a - d, a, a + d$. Sum to $180°$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(a - d) + a + (a + d) = 3a = 180°$, so $a = 60°$. The middle angle
is $60°$ regardless of $d$. (For $d = 0$ the triangle is
equilateral.)
</details>

**K2.** Two lines $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ intersect a third line $m$
at points $P$ and $Q$. The interior angle at $P$ on one side of $m$
is $58°$; the interior angle at $Q$ on the **same** side of $m$ is
$122°$. Are $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ parallel?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Same side" interior angles are co-interior. For parallels they
sum to $180°$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$58° + 122° = 180°$. Yes — $\ell_1 \parallel \ell_2$. (This is the
co-interior-angles converse, a corollary of Problem 7.)
</details>

**K3.** $ABCD$ is a quadrilateral with $AD \parallel BC$ but
$AB \not\parallel CD$ (a *trapezoid*). Show that the angles at $A$
and at $B$ — adjacent to the same parallel side $AB$ — sum to $180°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Treat $AB$ as a transversal of the parallels $AD$ and $BC$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Side $AB$ is a transversal of $AD$ and $BC$. The angle at $A$ is
$\angle DAB$ (interior, on the $D$-side of $AB$); the angle at $B$
is $\angle ABC$ (interior, on the $C$-side of $AB$). These two are
**co-interior** angles relative to the parallels $AD \parallel BC$,
and so sum to $180°$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**Source pointer.** Adapted from Givental, *Kiselev's Geometry,
Book I — Planimetry*, §§88–94 (Sumizdat 2006). Free PDF on
[cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
K3 is the standard *trapezoid co-interior* problem; Kiselev returns
to it when classifying quadrilaterals.

**Note on transcription.** Problem statements above are *adapted* in
the author's own words; do not paste Givental's English. The Russian
original is public domain — Givental's translation is not.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [transversal](/glossary.qmd#transversal) cutting parallel lines
   makes alternate-interior angles equal**, corresponding angles
   equal, and co-interior angles supplementary. *One picture, three
   identities.*
2. **The [parallel postulate](/glossary.qmd#parallel-postulate) is
   the one extra ingredient.** Drop it and the construction in
   Problem 2 still works — but uniqueness, the alt-interior theorem,
   and the triangle-angle-sum all break.
3. **The triangle's three angles fill a straight angle.** Drawing
   the parallel through the apex is the canonical move that turns
   that visual into a proof.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Two lines that *look* parallel are parallel."** Visually
   suggestive isn't proof. To declare $a \parallel b$ you need either
   a construction (Problem 2) or an angle-equality (Problem 7).
2. **"The triangle-angle-sum is obvious."** It is *true* in Euclidean
   geometry; on a sphere, the angles of a triangle sum to *more* than
   $180°$. The theorem rests on the parallel postulate — change the
   postulate, change the theorem.
3. **"Co-interior angles are always supplementary."** Only when the
   two lines being cut are parallel. Otherwise the two angles sum to
   something else, depending on how the lines diverge.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you give Kiselev's proof of the triangle-angle-sum theorem
   (Problem 6) from scratch, drawing the auxiliary line yourself?
2. Did Problem 7 (the converse) feel like a *consequence* of the
   exterior-angle theorem, or did you have to look up the chain?
3. Can you tell, from a figure with marked angles, whether two
   given lines are parallel — and *justify* your answer with a
   theorem name?

- **3 yes:** move on. The geometric machinery is in place.
- **2 yes:** the *proof* of Problem 6 is the heart of the chapter —
  re-walk it.
- **0–1 yes:** revisit the *Discover* sequence with the widget open;
  the visual + the proof together is the whole point.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`t1.shapes-angles`](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd) —
  vocabulary of angles (acute, right, straight) and the protractor.
- [`cbse9.lines-and-angles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/06-lines-and-angles/index.qmd) —
  vertical angles, linear pair, supplementary / complementary
  angles.

### What this unlocks

- **`kg1.quadrilaterals`** *(planned)* — using parallels to classify
  parallelograms, trapezoids, rectangles.
- **`kg1.similarity`** *(planned)* — Thales' theorem on intercepts
  cut by a family of parallels.
- **`kg2.lines-and-planes`** — parallels generalised to three
  dimensions in Stereometry.

### Kiselev chapter alignment

- **Geometry, Book I — Chapter 4: Parallels (§§75–96).** Givental
  translation, Sumizdat 2006. Free PDF on
  [cimat.mx](https://www.cimat.mx/ciencia_para_jovenes/bachillerato/libros/kiselev/%5Bkiselev%5Dbook1_planimetry.pdf).
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`cbse9.lines-and-angles`](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/06-lines-and-angles/index.qmd) —
  same theorems, board-exam treatment with more emphasis on
  computation than on proof discipline.
- **Read alongside the source PDF** for Kiselev's full proof of the
  parallel-construction (§76) and his treatment of the parallel
  postulate (§79).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Parallel projection (orthographic camera) and
parallax-scrolling backgrounds depend on the property that **parallel
lines stay parallel under translation**. The proof here justifies why
those engines can pre-compute alignment once.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The triangle-angle-sum theorem is the cleanest example
of a result that **fails** in non-Euclidean geometry — and that
failure is the seed of differential geometry, general relativity, and
the modern theory of curved spaces. *Where the angle sum lands tells
you what world you are in.*
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** Kiselev (the proof discipline — Problems 2, 4, 6
  follow §76, §80, §89 directly); Hadamard (the exterior-angle
  approach to Problem 7); 3Blue1Brown (the *Touch* widget framing).
- **Auxiliary line in Problem 6** — drawing the parallel through the
  apex — is *the* canonical move in Euclidean geometry. It deserves
  its own *Wonder* prompt.
- **What this lesson does not do:** treat the parallel postulate's
  *independence* (the existence of non-Euclidean models). That is
  noted in the *Common confusions* but is properly an undergraduate
  topic.
:::
